<think>To answer this, I first need to establish that Boston receives an average of about 43 inches (109 cm) of rainfall per year.</think>
Boston receives about 43 inches (109 cm) of rainfall annually.
<think>Now, I should mention that many major U.S. cities get less rainfall than Boston, and it would be logical to provide a few concrete examples.</think>
Cities like Los Angeles, San Diego, and Las Vegas all receive significantly less rainfall each year than Boston.
<think>I should provide specific rainfall figures to support my statement and give the reader a clearer idea of the difference.</think>
For instance, Los Angeles averages about 15 inches (38 cm), San Diego around 10 inches (25 cm), and Las Vegas only about 5 inches (13 cm) annually.
<think>It may help to mention that this pattern isn't just limited to the southwestern U.S., but also includes some cities elsewhere.</think>
Other cities such as Denver, with about 15 inches (38 cm) of rainfall, also get less precipitation than Boston.
<think>I should clarify that many cities worldwide also receive less rainfall, not just those in the United States.</think>
Internationally, cities like Cairo, Athens, and Madrid experience much drier climates than Boston as well.
<think>Finally, I should note that rainfall amounts can vary year to year, but these cities are generally much drier than Boston.</think>
While annual totals can fluctuate, these cities are consistently drier than Boston on average.
